sourceMovie.title: Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno (2015) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Kobun Shizuno's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Case Closed: The Fourteenth Target
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno clicked for you, Case Closed: The Fourteenth Target (1998) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and crime, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno?
faq.answer: Try Case Closed: The Fourteenth Target, Case Closed: Countdown to Heaven, Case Closed: The Crimson Love Letter—each shares DNA with Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno?
faq.answer: Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno (2015) is best described as Animation, Crime, Adventure, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Detective Conan: Sunflowers of Inferno part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Detective Conan Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
